item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

The user TobiasTheTapir has written an excellent tale featuring this SCP, all about the challenges of trying to contain it, called The Playful Beast. I feel that an inline link could easily be added to this article leading to said tale. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Provisional Containment Zone Delta-3 has been constructed surrounding SCP-2301’s manifestation area. At any given time, no fewer than 2 containment teams (designated Alpha and Beta) must be present, with a third relief team on active standby.

To this:

Provisional Containment Zone Delta-3 has been constructed surrounding SCP-2301’s manifestation area. At any given time, no fewer than 2 containment teams (designated Alpha and Beta) must be present, with a third relief team on active standby.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as far as I'm aware, what you described is a wyvren, not a dragon

From: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wyvern
The wyvern has often been confused with the dragon, due to the similarities between them and due to the wyvern being a lesser-known mythical creature. In the Middle Ages, no clear distinction was made between the two. Since the sixteenth century, in English, Scottish, and Irish heraldry, the key distinction has been that a wyvern has two legs, whereas a dragon has four; however, this distinction is not generally observed in the heraldry of other European countries, where two-legged dragons are entirely acceptable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that this could use more detail, also as far as I'm aware, what you described is a wyvren, not a dragon

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded: Reports of my death have been greatly exaggerated.

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded: Psst, the post was made in 2014, and the user isn't that active as much anymore.

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded: TELL ME YOUR WAYS, COLDPOST MASTER

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And Charlie Team gets laser hand beams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd assume it means "having inimical tendencies towards". "Inimical" means "causing harm or destruction". So I'd assume inimity would mean something like "the goal of causing lots of destruction". It doesn't seem like it's a real word, strangely enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Translator from the Korean Wiki. What does the word 'inimity' stand for? I cannot grasp the meaning of the word from any dictionaries available.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 This SCiP is fun regardless of chronometric-traits-not-to-be-discussed. But I do agree with others that it seems like a weak delivery for such a strong premise.
A couple thoughts: It seems like SCP-2301 presents a culturally appropriate manifestation. For this reason, as the Foundation brought in a containment team of mixed cultural origin, it started using the dominant European mythic threat. If this is right, they might include restrictions on assigned personnel to limit -2301 from further expanding its repitoire. Perhaps more importantly, they might want to keep such personnel socially segregated from the rest of the Foundation. If this thing dug into the institutional culture of the Foundation, and picked the most mythic Keter, the best reasonable hope might be manifesting as SCP-682.
Personally, I think it would manifest as SCP-083-D, and require the "hero" be RIDING SCP-682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 Also, we are done the minute this thing gets word of the daevites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting and pretty fun to read.

It also make me think…if the Foundation has to keep update containment procedures, so they will eventually have more than dozen teams, each represent different cultures (Chinese team, Celtic team, Native American team (possibly Cowboy team as well, just in case), Indian team, and so on… XD ).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure speaking English count as "breaking character" when perform Kabuki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I Fought the Lore?

I know this was posted in April, but that is really funny. I'm surprised weizhong didn't go with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When it's an oni, do the agents have to speak in Japanese? Or even in 15th century Japanese? If so, that should be noted in the containment section. If not, the description section should mention that it doesn't care about the language used, even though it cares about everything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree that containment procedures seem…rather inadequate. Expanding on how it gathers its information could add some creep factor, like many others said this has some nice potential routes it could develop. Add a interview or some sort of communication to offer some tidbit explaining its preference for in character assassination. I can't imagine many sane human being saying 'Oh hey there looks like our guns aren't doing any damage on that there dragon lets dress up as knights and hope that helps'

Seems like the trial and error to come to that conclusion would leave plenty of room for mass destruction, and where does it exactly stop the killing, does it have some arbitrary body or village count it needs to reach before it tires? Or does it attempt to act out the play to a conclusion. There are quite a few holes in this article that could use something besides a 'dunno' blanket covering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, and for a 49 minute write up it certainly isn't bad at all. I just have two questions - first, how did the foundation discover the method for killing this thing? I imagine at first typical capture techniques were tried and failed. The skip then probably moved to the nearest town and begun killing everyone in sight… but who came up with the idea to dress agents up as samurai? How was its weakness uncovered?
Second, how quickly could the foundation come up with a counter to a third form? You said it's mostly versed in Japanese and Germanic cultures (how did they find that out as well?), but what's stopping this thing from coming back as the aliens from independence day? Or the Cloverfield monster?
Again, you did some absolutely excellent work under an hour, and I'm excited to see which direction you take this thing, if any. Keep up the good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tried to address some of these concerns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty dang good. +1
TOTPD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I hate how it was implemented.

We have intricate and details instructions on how to temporarily destroy 2301, and if anything goes wrong, it will be that much harder to destroy. However, I didn't find why it had to be destroyed once it manifested. Would it not be much simpler to just contain it once manifested instead of repeatedly killing it over and over?

And in the event that the creature manifests in a form that's different from the current two, how is the team supposed to equip itself that quickly? It would take a while to determine what location and period the monster is from, get the right equipment, find and/or train people to speak and act the right parts, then equip them.

I also felt that if 2301 were to speak, it would behave like a five-year-old.

Agent Z: I stabbed you in the heart! You should be fatally wounded!

SCP-2301: Nuh-uh! You costume isn't right so it doesn't count!

Z: What's wrong with my costume?

2301: The crest on your shield is French, not German!

Z: A Frenchman can be in Germany! The armor is in the right time period and everything!

2301: Well you just broke character so-

SCP-2301 sticks its tongue out at Agent A.

Again, I like the idea, but it still needs heluva lot of work. Honestly, I'm surprised it has that high of a score.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad at all. I agree it feels like it's missing some measure of depth, but I'm not quite sure what. Perhaps some theorizing by Research, or another form or two (tanuki and kitsune come to mind), and warnings to keep extraneous reading and research material outside of the manifestation range — we don't know how it learned about European dragons, but there's no sense taking chances on it manifesting as, say, Slender Man or Cthulhu or anything of that sort.

One minor quibble: You give the "dragon" form winged forelimbs and clawed hind limbs. That's a wyvern; a European dragon is a hexapod, having four legs and a pair of wings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems to qualify for Keter from this angle. It's a metamorphic being that can only be injured by "appropriate" weapons wielded by "appropriate" people acting "appropriately". If it shows up as a Japanese [I]oni[/I], nothing but katanas wielded by people dressed and acting like samurai will work; if a European dragon, nothing but medieval weaponry wielded by people dressed and acting like medieval knights will work. Break character before it's dead and gone and the kill doesn't count; break character in battle, you can't touch it. (But it can touch YOU, and is just as capable as its legendary counterpart.) And if the thing starts dragging in other mythologies… well, things could get interesting as Research scrambles to find the thing's new weakness before it reaches a city and starts killing people indiscriminately, completely invulnerable to anything outside its chosen mythos. Pray it doesn't find out about creepypastas… some of those are unkillable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2301: LARP like your life depends on it.

As others have said, this one could use a bit more development. I think it's good enough for an upvote as-is, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfectly fine. I'm just clearing up some misconceptions here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I´m sorry, the post is still to bland for my taste, and is too similar to the original dragon in this site to be noteworthy, its a good idea, but it needs a lot of work, I won´t change my vote because your explanation in the article leaves too much to be desired and lacks a sense of "uniqueness" I don´t think the tone is right and doesn´t do anything to me

I want to make it clear, it IS a good Idea, BUT, it NEEDS more work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciating the Classics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a good piece of work for 49 minutes, but not good enough for an upvote.

Though I am noticing a trend in the site of late of "this person is operating outside of the norm and taking a measure of pride in this. CRUCIFY THEM."

This concerns me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty neat, I think I'm in with the "it's missing something" crowd, though. I mean, for being a Keter, it doesn't leave a huge impression on me, despite the fact that I like the idea and the execution.

Speaking of which…

The paragraph starting "SCP-2301 will remain in character" is just a rehash of the containment procedures and can be struck. Everything that isn't directly stated in the containment is inferrable.

Also, the paragraph talking about how it initially appeared should be moved to below the two paragraphs about its various forms (perhaps make them an addendum?) I'd also suggest reducing it just to "we don't know if it can do anything else, but that's always possible".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is difficult to contain due to the particular measures needed in order to combat the thing. These guys need to stay in character as actors/actresses even though this skip is actively attempting to murder them. If this thing ever got out, it would cause massive havoc as all of the world's militaries tried to kill it with their normal armaments, only to spectacularly fail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is it Keter? This seems slightly harder than the average Euclid, but nothing that's going to destroy the world.

Otherwise, it's pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So many Keters lately :O

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Myth-Obsessed Monster? Folklore-Obsessed Fiend? Scripted Legend? A Creature From Legend? Mythological Roleplayer (if the backstory is indeed that something is roleplaying these things)? Odious Oni, Dreadful Dragon, and Friends? I Fought the Lore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I stated in the discussion page:

I found the lack of a proper setting to be redacted kind of dull considering that it would be in the best interest of the foundation to have people where it is exactly, a whole document which only the place is redacted seems like an easy job to cover up a lazy mind that just won´t name a place for the sake of lazyness

It's the name of a place. The actual location of it isn't important unless you're actively looking for its containment zone. If you needed to know where it was located, you wouldn't see a blackbox.

why doesn´t the thing try to escape?

This is stated in the article. It can't really escape, because outside of its manifestation event, it's not really around. When it does manifest, it immediately tries to escape.

Why do we care!? what would it do if we don't intervene!?

Also stated in the article. It's highly aggressive, and will try to head towards the nearest large population center.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found the lack of a proper setting to be redacted kind of dull considering that it would be in the best interest of the foundation to have people where it is exactly, a whole document which only the place is redacted seems like an easy job to cover up a lazy mind that just won´t name a place for the sake of lazyness.

the concept is cool enough but there are too many similarities with our resident dragon

why doesn´t the thing try to escape?

why does the thing wants to be killed by era accurate enemies??

Why do we care!? what would it do if we don't intervene!?

its just too vague and bland that it seems like a waste of effort, and too similar to other Skips that it just doesn´t stand out enough…

- vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This line of conversation is done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the majority of the users like and upvote them, then why not?

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
Focus on the piece and not the process, please.

The process has affected the piece. In my opinion, that makes my criticism valid and relevant to the discussion, as well as within the bounds of the Policy. As I've detailed in excruciating length above.

I'll shut up if that's indeed the staff order. But I don't think the line is as clear as you're presenting it here.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: Please read the below mod post. I understand you were writing this while I posted mine, but we're leaving the topic alone from here on out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The classic Thing that does a Thing SCP is basically that it does X under Y circumstances, with little other information not directly related to that or its containment procedure.

This thing either becomes a Dragon or Oni, and requires a special task force to defeat it while dressed as either Knights or Samurai, or it goes on a rampage. It has no testing logs, no interview, no associated tales, no [REDACTED] or [DATA EXPUNGED], no interface screw, no horrible implications written between the lines… it just does X under Y circumstances, and we're given all the information needed to understand what it does and why it needs the Special Containment Procedures.

That's why I call it a classic Thing that does a Thing SCP. Of course, that's just my opinion of what makes a classic Thing that does a Thing SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I'm not sure why I've been singled out for this kind of treatment, given that many authors on the site have coldposted before, and one major contributor's portfolio consists of many coldposts.

You seem to be under the impression that you're being criticized merely for the act of coldposting. That is incorrect, and you'd know that's incorrect if you read my earlier criticism. My issue is that you're blaring out in the comments to anyone who will listen that you wrote this in 49 minutes. Which is in effect blatantly advertising to all of us that you prioritized finishing the article as quickly as possible ahead of actually making it good. Also, if you're going to cite "major contributors" as comparison cases, you should go all the way with that and lay your cards on the table.

I can assure you that whenever I see this in the future, I'll give whoever's doing it the same criticism. I've refrained from doing this in the past for various reasons, but I'm pretty tired of it now. You're certainly not the first, but from here on out I'll be delivering this same critique whenever I see it.

A coldpost is an "achievement" of sorts, and I suppose that you could make that comparison. But saying that is like saying marathon runners do what they do in order to tell people that they've run a marathon. No, the reason that they run marathons is because it's a personal test.

Coldposting is merely one method of writing a draft. Some people like to have readers intervene at various stages in the writing process. Others like to write a draft, let it sit, come back to it and revise it, repeat ad nauseum, and then internalize critique to either fine-tune the existing product or incorporate the concepts into the next project. Others have still other methods.

Leaving aside the "coldpost as achievement" discussion, if you want to make a marathon analogy, coldposting and then advertising it as blatantly as has been done here is like running the marathon, taking four selfies at the finish line to tweet to the world, and then posting multiple Facebook status updates on how you've just run a marathon.

I am perfectly fine with the members of this site disagreeing with the quality of the work itself. I am not fine with people attempting to turn this into a personal attack on my image as a writer and my respect for the writing standards on this site.

Your method has affected the quality of your work. I'm not the only person who's said that. Therefore your advertised intent in writing this is fair game. No one is attacking your "image".

Coldposting is the ultimate test of writing ability.

You keep saying that. See above.

It is not saying that this article is superior to the higher quality articles on this site that took months of work, anymore than an artist who practices speed-painting is claiming their superiority to the works of Van Gogh or Raphael.

You're submitting works that are to be considered with the same critical eye that we give to everything else of note on the wiki. This is why we constantly tell new members that it doesn't matter if they're new, their work is judged by the same merits. I care not in the slightest if there was some sort of external constraint that reduced the quality of the piece. It's still a piece of reduced quality. That you advertised it as flagrantly as you did is what drew my response, both because I thought you were putting this whole "coldposting is badass" business ahead of doing something good, and because I wish to discourage this sort of stuff in the future.

item.title: Mod Post--Stop Order
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm gonna go ahead and say everybody can chill out about the fact that it's a coldpost1. Weizhong wrote the article he wanted to have, and it happened to take 49 minutes. For some people, it takes longer. For some people, it doesn't. Technically, complaining about the fact that he said something about his writing process isn't critiquing the piece, it's critiquing the author's commentary on the piece.

His decision to discuss his writing process does not open him up to any special critique on that front; all comments made should adhere to Guideline 5 of the Criticism Policy that Kalinin helpfully linked above, which says:

5. Don't try to enforce your personal preferences as How-Things-Should-Be-Done: There's no rule saying someone must use a sandbox, or swear off writing humanoids/-Js/Keters until they're more experienced, or that Author Avatars are the cancer that is killing SCP. If a new writer wants to try something risky, let them go for it. The worst that can happen is that their article gets deleted. We want people being ambitious and taking chances.

That includes how much time is taken to write the article. Focus on the piece and not the process, please.

Footnotes
1. EDIT: That means both sides are done talking about it here. Take it to PMs or a general forum thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I would like to apologize to anybody who interpreted this as showboating. This was not my intention at all.

My intention was to write a coldpost. And I'm not sure why I've been singled out for this kind of treatment, given that many authors on the site have coldposted before, and one major contributor's portfolio consists of many coldposts.

A coldpost is an "achievement" of sorts, and I suppose that you could make that comparison. But saying that is like saying marathon runners do what they do in order to tell people that they've run a marathon. No, the reason that they run marathons is because it's a personal test.

Thus, the coldpost. Writing a coldpost is a test of author skill. Like the many other authors who write coldposts, I wanted to test my ability to crank out a quality piece of writing in a brief amount of time. I am perfectly fine with the members of this site disagreeing with the quality of the work itself. I am not fine with people attempting to turn this into a personal attack on my image as a writer and my respect for the writing standards on this site.

Coldposting is the ultimate test of writing ability. It's the challenge of writing something of acceptable quality in a brief amount of time. It is not saying that this article is superior to the higher quality articles on this site that took months of work, anymore than an artist who practices speed-painting is claiming their superiority to the works of Van Gogh or Raphael.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's arguably okay to do something like coldpost just for the sake of personal curiosity (i.e Can I pull this off?). If an author wants to publish a work that's likely not as good as it could be, then that's their loss.

Edit: To clarify, I support Weizhong's choice to do this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, don't be a sourpuss.

I'll express my criticism within the bounds of the Criticism Policy as I goddamn well please, thanks.

It's fun to put a time limit on yourself and see if you can crank out a good finished product in an extremely small amount of time

It's fun to do lots of things. That doesn't mean they should all be posted as finished works of fiction to be read and critiqued.

I think that if Weizhong is being cocky, it is righteously so, because being able to write a skip in 49 minutes and have it accrue major upvotes is a pretty cool thing to be capable of.

I obviously disagree about how "righteous" this is, but this is exactly what I'm talking about. I don't like "I'm going to do this thing and be an SCP BADASS!1!" as the primary motivation for writing, when it comes at the expense of "I'm going to write something good".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not entirely agreeing with you, but I think what bothers me the msot is that this could have been far better (as everyone said). Luckily, I do have an idea f what's lacking on it.

First off, this is just a bare description of the thing (wich is not abd and got upvoted anyways, but it's not enough for me) and what it does. Maybe this has an actual story behind, but you wrote it so fast, you didn't give yourself time to develope it. An "interview" (or int his case, a log of the agents fighting and trash talking at the skip) would have been really good to understand what this skip wants to achieve. Where does the skip manifest? Is it a fixed place or is it wherever it wants? What are its reasons to behave in this way? Is it an Epic actor or just a myth fanboy? Those are all questions that you would have answered if you took the time to develope this into something marvelous instead of a kinda bland article.

Too abd, had potential. It's relatively well written, but I won't upvote it.

E:Man, totally ninj'd!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stuff like this bothers me. It's basically saying "fuck artistic merit, I'm going to Do a Thing because I can!". So you wrote up something in 49 minutes because…why, exactly

No. This is an exercise in showboating. Which is why you apparently felt the need to advertise how awesome you are in all caps as the first post in the thread. And, to be perfectly frank, if your intent is to show off, this ain't the piece to do it with.

I sort of agree with you. In particular it bothers me when people do that, in part because it seems like a cop-out so that if the article fails, they can say "Well I shat it out in under an hour, what'd you expect?" and if it succeeds they can say "Well I shat it out in under an hour, imagine what awesomeness I could make if I put actual effort into it!" and I've known people like that who were really awful people.

As for the article itself… it's incredibly boring. It's an anomalous roleplayer hitting all the stereotypical "internet tough guy historian" clichés; Japanese mythology and folklore, and Norse/Germanic mythology and folklore because dude, samurais and Teutonic Knights and fuckin Vikings brah

And it appears to be Keter because you can't contain the dragon, breh!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww, don't be a sourpuss. It's fun to put a time limit on yourself and see if you can crank out a good finished product in an extremely small amount of time—and it's damned hard to do, especially when there are great writers on the site who spent months working on one skip. I think that if Weizhong is being cocky, it is righteously so, because being able to write a skip in 49 minutes and have it accrue major upvotes is a pretty cool thing to be capable of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, first off, this strikes me as SCP-453 except without any of the depth that article had. In this one, it's just a super-indestructible monster what kills you if the scripts aren't followed. It doesn't do anything for me.

Mostly, though, my problem is this:

WELCOME TO WEIZHONG'S COLDPOST THEATER.

This was written in 49 minutes.

Stuff like this bothers me. It's basically saying "fuck artistic merit, I'm going to Do a Thing because I can!". So you wrote up something in 49 minutes because…why, exactly? This piece achieved perfection in precisely 49 minutes? It couldn't have possibly been improved by you going over it again and tweaking it or revising it in any way?

No. This is an exercise in showboating. Which is why you apparently felt the need to advertise how awesome you are in all caps as the first post in the thread. And, to be perfectly frank, if your intent is to show off, this ain't the piece to do it with.

Lots of people around here coldpost. It's an individual decision whether that's how your process works best. And that's fine. But I take writing for this site very, very seriously. It's an island of standards in a sea of internet bullshit, and I dislike it very, very much when writers rush out a piece with the primary focus being on how they've checked off a thing on their list of Awesome Things To Do, rather than giving us good fiction. People expend real effort and real time in contributing around here, and this "I banged something out that's just good enough to stick to the wiki because ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED" business feels disrespectful to those that actually take this process seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, I think I remember seeing you mention you were doing an SCP where the guards had to wield katanas. Glad to see it turned out well. I like it :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm hard pressed to think of a single SCP that isn't, somewhere along the line, a Thing that does a Thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Matching Game RPG?

Matching Game Role-Playing Game…

Something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any suggestions for a title are welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could do that long before you ever did.

Also, what have I created???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A hundred successful articles frown upon that statement, Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… really really cool. But damnit if you can make an SCP in under an hour, then what makes me special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh.
Uh.

So… what if one day SCP-2301 got tired of the usual medieval fantasy stuff and decides to do sci-fi instead? Now what would the Foundation do?

Why, hire Tom Cruise, of course.

But he might die!

Might he, now… ? /sly smile

Without reliance on other skips, the task force personnel would need to improvise with current laser and walker tech and stuff like that, or they could get lucky if normal guns are sufficient to stop SCP-2301 which would made the whole thing easier to contain than with katanas, though those guns would have to be modified to look like typical sci fi video game guns to work. Or they could use 'futuristic' drones armed with lasers.

Depending on the version of the story, which might become a tough problem here. That is… is SCP-2301 representing a Star Trek-type fantasy, where a yellow or blue suit may solve the problem but a red one is an almost certain death warrant? Or is it representing a Star Wars-type story, where it will be beaten by Ewan McGregor with a beard and a glowing stick? Or a Farscape story? Or a Galactica story? Or a DC/Marvel one? Or a bloody Angel from NGE? Would we need an EVA in that last scenario? To us, it might be obvious. To a researcher, and to the guys waiting there with armors and swords, it would not.

Let's just say that making it stick to stereotypical "traditional" settings might be easier to sort out. I wonder what was it doing before entering containment, though…

A lulz ending can also be used in which SCP-2301 appears as a monster that can be defeated with normal modern military weapons like nukes and the like. A Foundation researcher will also make the suggestion to decrease the class level down to safe if the skip decides to stay modern indefinitely.

… having to use nukes to contain a thing that can become whatever it "wants" does not sound like a safe-class skip.

Anyways, sorry. Liked it, Weizhong. This makes my fun sense tingle. : D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Removing the reference to Beowulf.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… what if one day SCP-2301 got tired of the usual medieval fantasy stuff and decides to do sci-fi instead? Now what would the Foundation do?

Without reliance on other skips, the task force personnel would need to improvise with current laser and walker tech and stuff like that, or they could get lucky if normal guns are sufficient to stop SCP-2301 which would made the whole thing easier to contain than with katanas, though those guns would have to be modified to look like typical sci fi video game guns to work. Or they could use 'futuristic' drones armed with lasers.

Like the previous comments had mentioned, this skip has a lot of potential waiting to be exploited, like more detail and story especially concerning a typical containment 'battle' and also other genres can be dealt with like sci fi and stuff.

A lulz ending can also be used in which SCP-2301 appears as a monster that can be defeated with normal modern military weapons like nukes and the like. A Foundation researcher will also make the suggestion to decrease the class level down to safe if the skip decides to stay modern indefinitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a nice classic SCP, and I can respect that. It's pretty good for something that you coldposted after 49 minutes.

However, I can't upvote because there are a number of inconsistencies here. Firstly, how can the containment team pin down the dragon as specifically coming from Beowulf? The poem doesn't describe the dragon's appearance clearly enough to distinguish it from other dragons.

Secondly, as we know it is copying the dragon from Beowulf, Beta team is improperly equipped to deal with it and the dragon should rightly kill them, since they aren't in the right role. The reason for this is that they are attired in 15th-Century German plate armour - Beowulf was written, at the very least, 200 years before plate armour became a thing, so plate armour should be unrecognizable to the dragon. Ring-mail, round shields and viking-style helmets and weapons are the order of the day if it's manifesting as the dragon from Beowulf.

The poem is also set in Scandinavia and composed in Britain, so the repeated references to how well the entity understands Medieval German culture, while not necessarily nonsensical, do seem a bit odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Didn't really do anything for me.
